title: Forget banks, super funds are now too big to fail
full_text:
A run on each of the nation’s four big banks; an economy sliding into recession; a collapse in the dollar combined with a major tech outage. It’s the stuff of nightmares, particularly for financial regulators.
It is also Australia’s worst liquidity shock in 50 years – and it was the hypothetical scenario set down by regulators to stress test the nation’s financial system.
This is the first time the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority tested banks and super funds together as an interconnected system.
The good news is the system passed – just, and with significant caveats. The extreme hypothetical shock thrown at banks and super funds exposed some notable fault lines, and that was very much the intention of the test.
The intensive test isn’t so much designed for a pass or fail, it was more about where the risks in the system are lurking.
The backdrop to the test is rising interest rates and an environment where surging AI valuations are also seen as adding risks to the financial system.
This week the Swiss-based Bank of International Settlements warned an AI-investment bust could have knock-on effects for the financial system.
The key finding is the deep ties between super funds and banks through funding, deposits and equity which can now amplify shocks through the financial system as readily as they can absorb them.
Banks with a high concentration of super-sourced funding face greater risk.
Bringing the big four banks and six large, unnamed super funds under a single stress test also underscores the rise of super as a systemic force in Australia.
The pool of super savings – now exceeding $4 trillion – is just short of the total assets in the nation’s banking system. In the coming decade, super assets are likely to overtake banks and will be a multiple of Australia’s GDP.
Many funds carry large investment portfolios alongside relatively limited balance sheets, and rely heavily on external managers and outsourcing. This makes them even more vulnerable during times of crisis, according to APRA.
But the rise of mega-funds, nearly all industry funds with more than $100bn in investments has created concentration risk. AustralianSuper is the biggest with more than $400bn in super savings under management. Unions behind industry funds argue APRA’s push over the past decades to force smaller funds (sub $30bn) to merge has created concentration risk its warning about today.
During the stress test – which included GDP contracting by 2 per cent – deposits from super funds sitting in banks fell by almost 40 per cent.
Notably, the top risk officers at banks and super funds held different assumptions around how quickly funding would be pulled in a crisis.
These differences in assumptions could seriously aggravate stress management.
In response, banks would be forced to slash lending while leaning on the Reserve Bank and tapping global money markets.
Remarkably, even under their own stress, super funds showed a willingness to still provide equity capital to banks. This is a recognition that a big bank failure could have a catastrophic effect on the economy and result in even greater losses for super fund members.
Under the test, average member balances fell 25 per cent. To meet liquidity demands – mostly driven by member switching – funds sold $81bn worth of shares and $11bn in bonds, in both cases this was overwhelmingly offshore assets.
Cumulative liquidity calls on the six super funds totalled $174bn, or just under 16 per cent of their net assets. Two funds indicated they would consider introducing a buy-sell spread for member switching – essentially an exit fee designed to slow the pace of outflows. An extreme event would likely see a freeze in member assets.
The large holdings of shares which could be liquidated in days offered an important funding source for the super funds. However it would no doubt incur heavy losses on portfolios. During the test, funds deliberately avoided selling unlisted assets (property, infrastructure, private equity), leaving their remaining portfolios heavily skewed to unlisted assets as equity holdings shrunk.
Some funds flagged they would defer or reduce commitments to private credit and those same unlisted sectors to rebuild their balance sheets, potentially creating further market stress in the process. Super funds are also big buyers of government bonds and if they sit out of the market, funding costs could rise further.
As the test played out, banks were forced to raise around $40bn in new equity, at a significant discount to market prices, reflecting simultaneous raisings, likely ratings downgrades and broader stress in the system. Super funds were prepared to tip in as much as $7bn, reinforcing their role as a source of domestic capital.
This first came to the attention of regulators during the global financial crisis, when the big super funds opened up liquidity lines for domestic banks just as global money markets started freezing over.
It proved to be a major shock absorber for local lenders compared to their global rivals at the height of the crisis. Now the reliance by banks on super is even deeper, and that means both sectors have become too big to fail.
johnstone@theaustralian.com.au
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
